title: Grass Stain on Cotton
stainChemistry: Grass is a three-component stain: chlorophyll (a fat-soluble magnesium-porphyrin pigment that gives grass its green color), plant proteins (RuBisCO and related enzymes crushed out during impact), and plant tannins (polyphenols common to most plant material). The chlorophyll is the visible green; the protein is the glue that holds it to the fiber; the tannin is the residual brown shadow after the green is gone. Cotton is pure cellulose — tolerates enzymes, IPA (isopropyl alcohol), NSD, and 3% H₂O₂ at room temperature. The one ordering constraint: protein before heat, or the stain sets.
whyThisWorks: Three chemistries, same ordering rule as every combination stain. Enzyme digests the plant protein that binds the chlorophyll to the fiber. IPA or Dawn (home) dissolves the chlorophyll once the protein matrix is broken — chlorophyll is fat-soluble, so water alone can't lift it. 3% H₂O₂ oxidizes the residual tannin shadow. Hot wash finishes. Reversing the order — peroxide before the protein is digested — leaves the chlorophyll locked behind the protein matrix and the whole treatment plateaus at faint green.
defaultAssumption: Fresh grass on cotton (kids' play clothes, sports uniforms) is a high-confidence DIY. Aged grass (days old) adds a peroxide cycle but stays in home scope. Heat-set grass (already hot-dried) is pro territory — the protein is cooked in.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold running water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold flush from the BACK of the fabric. Blot from underneath. Cold only — warm water sets the plant protein into cotton. You won't see much change yet — the chlorophyll stays in place because it's fat-soluble, not water-soluble.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B / protease digestant) or enzyme pre-soak in warm water (30–38°C)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply Protein Spotter directly or dissolve enzyme pretreat in warm water. The protease hydrolyzes the plant protein holding the chlorophyll in place. Brush gently with a soft bristle brush after the first 5 min. The stain doesn't visibly clear yet — it's preparing the chlorophyll for the next step.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Isopropyl alcohol 90%+ on a white cloth, OR NSD in warm water
spottingProtocol.instruction: IPA is the pro workhorse for chlorophyll — tamp from a white cloth, flip frequently. The green transfers to the cloth as the alcohol dissolves it. Cotton tolerates IPA without damage. If IPA isn't available, NSD does the same job more slowly — apply, work in with a soft brush, blot.
For the residual brown tannin shadow after the green is gone, apply 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop. Oxidizes the tannin chromophore. Dwell 3–5 min, blot. One oxidizer at a time.
Flush until rinse runs clear. Launder hot per care label. Safe at this point — protein is digested, so heat is on the finishing side.

steps: 1. Cold flush from the back — won't visibly change the green yet.
2. Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B / protease) in warm water (30–38°C). 15–20 min.
3. IPA (90%+) on a white cloth, tamp — chlorophyll is fat-soluble. OR NSD for a slower alternative.
4. 3% H₂O₂ on residual tannin shadow. 3–5 min. One pass.
5. NSD flush, hot wash per care label.
products: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B or protease digestant)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold flush; warm (30–38°C) for enzyme; cool through solvent + peroxide; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Never warm-water-flush fresh grass — sets the plant protein
Chlorophyll is fat-soluble — water won't touch it; IPA or surfactant required

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water From the Back First
homeSolutions.instruction: Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water for a minute. Cold only — warm water sets the plant protein that's holding the green pigment in place. You won't see much change yet because the chlorophyll doesn't rinse with water. That's expected. The next step handles the green.

Use an enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout). Dissolve in WARM water per the label — hot kills the enzymes. Apply to the stain, let sit 15–20 minutes. This breaks down the plant protein holding the chlorophyll to the fiber. The stain still looks green — that's fine, the next step pulls the green out.

homeSolutions.agent: Rubbing Alcohol or Dawn for the Green
homeSolutions.instruction: Grass green (chlorophyll) is fat-soluble — water alone can't move it, but alcohol or dish soap can. BEST: dab 70%+ rubbing alcohol (drugstore isopropyl) on a clean white cloth and press onto the stain. Flip the cloth frequently — green transfers out. GOOD: a drop of Dawn dish soap worked in with a soft brush for 2–3 minutes, rinsed cool. Cotton is fine with either.

Once the green is gone, a faint brown shadow may remain — that's the tannin the grass left behind. Dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on it, let it bubble for 3–5 min, blot. Skip on dark or dyed cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix hydrogen peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia — toxic fume risk.

homeSolutions.instruction: Flush cool water through the back of the fabric until the blotting towel comes out clean. No enzyme, alcohol, or peroxide residue before the wash.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows — safe now that protein is digested. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. Any residual stain sets permanently under dryer heat. If you still see green, repeat the alcohol / enzyme pass first.

If the grass has been ironed or hot-dried, or two full home rounds haven't cleared it, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Grass stain on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with enzyme pretreat + rubbing alcohol + 3% hydrogen peroxide. No heat until after enzyme."

steps: 1. Cold water flush from the BACK of the fabric — won't visibly change the green yet.
2. Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, Zout) dissolved in WARM water. Dwell 15–20 min.
3. Rubbing alcohol on a white cloth OR Dawn dish soap + soft brush — the step that actually pulls the green out. Cotton tolerates both.
4. 3% hydrogen peroxide on residual brown shadow. Skip on dark cotton.
6. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
7. Escalate if heat-set or two rounds failed.
products: Enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if grass has been hot-dried or ironed, two home rounds failed, or the cotton is dark dyed and peroxide isn't safe.
warnings: Cold water for the first flush — warm sets the plant protein
Enzymes die above 50°C — warm water, not hot
Chlorophyll is fat-soluble — water alone won't work; alcohol or Dawn required
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia

neverDo: Never warm-water-flush fresh grass — sets the plant protein
Never skip the enzyme step — chlorophyll stays locked behind the protein matrix
Never layer oxidizers — one at a time
Never dry with residual chemistry in the fiber
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates enzymes, IPA, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, and hot wash post-chemistry
Dyed cotton: skip or inside-seam-test peroxide
Heat-durable fiber once chemistry is cleared

materialWarnings: Protein first, green second, tannin shadow last
Chlorophyll needs alcohol or surfactant — water alone doesn't work
Peroxide on whites; test inside seam on dark cotton

escalation.when: Heat-set by iron or hot dryer, two home rounds failed, or dark dyed cotton where peroxide isn't safe.
escalation.whatToTell: "Grass stain on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with enzyme pretreat + rubbing alcohol + 3% hydrogen peroxide. [Hot-washed / not yet laundered.]"

contextQuestions: How old is the stain — fresh, overnight, days?
Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
Sports uniform or everyday garment? (sports uniforms often heat-dry routinely; caution)
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Chlorophyll fat-solubility is the main novelty — water alone stalls grass cleanup, which is why most failed home attempts plateau at faint green. Alcohol OR Dawn unlocks the rest of the protocol.
